ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Japanese short-grain cooked rice; closest defensible match is the generic cooked rice food product class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers cooked rice generically; no specific Japonica short-grain cooked rice code exists in the published FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for Japanese short-grain cooked rice; generic rice concepts exist but are not sufficiently specific to justify citation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single serving of Japanese rice is approximately 180 g (one rice bowl), providing around 295 kcal, 45 g carbohydrate, and 12 g protein.

nutrient_source.title: Japanese Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient data (G00010450)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database record G00010450 for Japanese Rice. The HPB record reports notably higher dietary fibre (3.7 g/100 g), protein (6.5 g/100 g), fat (4.21 g/100 g), sodium (231 mg/100 g), and iron (2.55 mg/100 g) compared to plain steamed white Japonica rice in USDA FoodData Central (USDA SR Legacy #20444: energy 130 kcal, protein 2.36 g, fat 0.19 g, carbohydrate 28.6 g, fibre 0.4 g, sodium 1 mg). This discrepancy strongly suggests the HPB record represents a mixed-grain or seasoned Japanese rice preparation rather than plain steamed white rice. Values are preserved as reported by HPB; a caveat is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for white short-grain rice, cooked
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value is available in the University of Sydney GI Database specifically for the HPB G00010450 Japanese Rice preparation. The GI of 72 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white short-grain rice (Japonica type), cooked, which is the closest defensible analogous food. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 45.3) / 100 ≈ 32.6, rounded to 32.5 for a 180 g serving (25.19 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 1.8 = 45.3 g). If this HPB record represents a mixed-grain preparation, actual GI may differ.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 231 mg/100 g is borderline elevated for a grain staple; a 180 g serving contributes approximately 416 mg sodium, which is 18–28% of the recommended daily limit of 1500–2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g cooked and avoid additional salty condiments such as soy sauce or miso; the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (327 mg K vs 231 mg Na per 100 g) partially offsets the sodium load per DASH dietary principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Japanese short-grain (Japonica) rice has a high glycemic index (GI ~72) and a 180 g serving delivers approximately 45 g of carbohydrate, producing a glycemic load of ~32.5, which is clinically significant for postprandial glucose management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict portion size to 100–150 g cooked rice per meal, pair with high-fibre vegetables and lean protein to blunt the glycaemic response, and monitor postprandial blood glucose; substituting partially with brown rice or barley can reduce the overall GI of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol is 0 mg/100 g (confirmed zero) and fat is 4.21 g/100 g; however, the sodium content (231 mg/100 g) and high glycaemic load are relevant cardiovascular risk factors, particularly for individuals with metabolic syndrome.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, limit serving size to 100–150 g, avoid high-sodium accompaniments, and combine with omega-3-rich fish and non-starchy vegetables to improve the overall cardiovascular risk profile of the meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Japanese Rice (日本のご飯; Nihon no gohan) refers to cooked short-grain Japonica rice, a staple of Japanese cuisine widely consumed in Singapore and across East and Southeast Asia. Japonica rice grains are short, round, and slightly sticky when cooked, producing a soft, moist, and mildly sweet texture that is distinct from long-grain varieties such as Thai jasmine or Indian basmati rice. The rice is typically prepared by washing, soaking, and steaming or boiling in a rice cooker. It forms the base of many Japanese dishes including sushi, donburi (rice bowls), onigiri (rice balls), and teishoku (set meals). The HPB Singapore Foodo database record (G00010450) reports energy of 164 kcal, protein 6.5 g, fat 4.21 g, carbohydrate 25.19 g, dietary fibre 3.7 g, and sodium 231 mg per 100 g. These values — particularly the elevated protein, fat, fibre, sodium, and iron (2.55 mg/100 g) — are substantially higher than plain steamed white Japonica rice (USDA SR Legacy: ~130 kcal, 2.4 g protein, 0.2 g fat, 28.6 g carbohydrate, 0.4 g fibre, 1 mg sodium per 100 g), suggesting this HPB record may represent a mixed-grain or seasoned preparation rather than plain white rice. Clinically, Japanese short-grain rice has a high glycaemic index (~72) and requires portion management for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The sodium content is borderline elevated and warrants caution for hypertensive individuals. Cholesterol is confirmed at 0 mg/100 g. The food is naturally gluten-free, vegan, halal, and kosher.

confidence_reason: The HPB Singapore Foodo database is an approved primary source and the record G00010450 is cited directly. However, the nutrient profile (particularly protein 6.5 g, fat 4.21 g, fibre 3.7 g, sodium 231 mg, iron 2.55 mg per 100 g) deviates substantially from all published values for plain steamed Japonica white rice in USDA FoodData Central and other approved sources, suggesting the HPB record may represent a mixed-grain or seasoned preparation. Because the exact preparation cannot be confirmed from the input record alone, confidence is rated medium rather than high.
notes: 1. NUTRIENT ANOMALY: The HPB G00010450 nutrient profile for 'Japanese Rice' is substantially higher in protein (6.5 g), fat (4.21 g), dietary fibre (3.7 g), sodium (231 mg), and iron (2.55 mg) per 100 g compared to plain steamed white Japonica rice in USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy #20444: protein 2.36 g, fat 0.19 g, fibre 0.4 g, sodium 1 mg, iron 0.2 mg per 100 g). This strongly suggests the HPB record represents a mixed-grain, fortified, or seasoned Japanese rice preparation. The HPB values are preserved as reported; downstream users should verify the exact preparation method. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data are not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this record, and no supplementary approved source was available to fill these fields for this specific preparation. 3. CHOLESTEROL: cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as reported by HPB for this record; plain cooked rice contains no cholesterol. 4. GI SOURCE: GI of 72 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white short-grain (Japonica) cooked rice; no direct GI measurement exists for the specific HPB G00010450 preparation. 5. CULTURAL NOTE: The input record lists primary_cuisine as 'Chinese' but the food name and food subgroup indicate Japanese cuisine; this record has been classified as Japanese cuisine in the output. 6. MULTILINGUAL SCOPE: As a Japanese dish, mandatory language coverage is Japanese (satisfied). Chinese simplified and traditional names are provided as supplementary context given the Singapore food database context and the input record's Chinese ethnic tag.
